entries.title: When Single-File HTML Is Insufficient, Adopt Stack B
entries.rationale:
The province pattern (single-file HTML PWA — SproutLab, SEP Dashboard, SEP Invoicing, Command Center) is the Order's default and should remain so when surface is small, interactivity is local to one screen, no cross-repo aggregation, no multi-route content, install-anywhere-zero-build is a virtue.

Stack B (SvelteKit 2 + adapter-static + Tailwind v4 + @vite-pwa/sveltekit + TypeScript strict + Biome + Vitest) is the right escalation when: cross-repo aggregation, content-heavy, long-lived with schema evolution, multi-route, rich interactivity. Temple is the founding exemplar.

Doctrine: don't modernize a province that doesn't need it. Don't keep a single-file HTML when scope has outgrown it. The tell: when your index.html crosses ~30K LOC or your split/ directory needs its own build pipeline docs, Stack B pays for itself.

entries.title: Observers Ship No Write Credentials — Edict III by Construction
entries.rationale:
Edict III (Sync Pipeline Authoritative) can be honored by policy ('don't write through a back door') or by construction ('no back door exists'). The latter is strictly stronger. Temple ships with no GitHub token, no API key, no write credentials of any kind; it can only read Codex JSON via anonymous raw.githubusercontent.com. Bypassing the sync pipeline would require code changes to Temple itself (visible in PR review).

Doctrine for all observer-class surfaces: close the write path at the architecture layer, not the process layer. Make the policy tautological.

entries.title: Novice-Friendliness Dominates Stack Selection in Early Projects
entries.rationale:
When the Builder is a novice, learning-cost dominates runtime performance cost. Astro + Svelte islands is technically-best for content-heavy dashboards (ships ~0 JS), but it requires holding two mental models — .astro frontmatter and .svelte island hydration. SvelteKit alone is one mental model end-to-end. The few kB of extra JS is invisible on 2026 networks.

Doctrine: for a novice Builder, pick the stack with fewer concepts, not the stack with better benchmarks. Migrate to the more-optimized stack later if needed — the rewrite is cheap; the learning debt is not.

entries.title: Charter Before Build, Always
entries.rationale:
Temple's README, CHARTER, and SCHEMA were drafted and reviewed before any Svelte component code. Edict VIII honored in letter and spirit. The practical payoff: every architectural question during scaffold had a pre-answered reference — the Charter itself. Zero mid-build rework on structural questions.

Doctrine: for every new Volume, the documents come before the code. If the README can't be written coherently, the code isn't ready to be written either.

entries.title: Stream Commits, Not Monoliths — Agent-Managed Scaffolding
entries.rationale:
Agent-driven scaffolding benefits from commit-streaming over monolithic pushes. Rationale: observable progress, resilience to stream timeouts, clearer commit history, atomic scope per commit. For Temple (41 files, ~70KB), 7 batches landed reliably after first monolithic attempt timed out twice.

Doctrine: batch agent commits by semantic unit — configs, app shell, types, components, routes, docs. Under ~10 files or ~15KB content each. Reviewer sees a readable story; agent sees observable progress.

entries.title: Watchtower Sibling Class to Monument — Observe vs Direct
entries.rationale:
Command Center is the Order's first Monument. Temple of Mars is its complement: where Command Center directs (decrees, handoffs), Temple observes (state, telemetry, lore). The functional split is real and worth formalizing. Proposed class: Watchtower — observational counterpart to Monument.

Doctrine (pending Working Committee ratification): structural classes are tools for clarifying role, not prestige. Watchtowers observe without directing; Monuments direct without necessarily observing; Provinces build. Each has its role, Builder seat, governance path.

entries.title: Branch + PR Flow Even for One-Line Hotfixes
entries.rationale:
During the Temple deploy fire-drill, direct push to main was tempting for each tiny fix. Held to branch + PR flow anyway. Payoff: each fix has a PR with a commit message, a testable description, a merge SHA. Post-mortem clarity is free. PR overhead in seconds; clarity in weeks of future work.

Doctrine: branch + PR for every change. No exceptions for one-line fixes, no exceptions for 'urgent' hotfixes. The ratification trail is what makes post-war lore synthesis possible.
